docs.title: χαῖρε!
docs.text: Logion is an open-source, no-code application designed to assist philologists using state-of-the-art language models. With Logion, researchers can download a suite of pre-modern Greek langauge models for conducting philological research tasks, such as error word detection and gap filling. Logion is designed to be run on personal computers without access to high performance computing (HPC) resources.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Logion is available for Windows and MacOS. We hope to make the application publicly available on our website soon.

For the time being, only researchers who have been provided a Google Drive link can download the Logion beta application. To do so, click the version of Logion corresponding to your device's operating system ("logion-Win" for Windows, "logion-mac" for MacOS).

After the .zip file has downloaded, double-click the .zip file in your Downloads folder. Open the resulting folder, and double-click the "Logion" application. This will open and install Logion on our system.

Note that, if your institution restricts downloads, you may need to contact your IT administrator to enable downloading new programs temporarily on your device.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text: Logion requires no outside software or installations.

docs.title: Acknowledgements
docs.text: Logion is developed through collaboration with Princeton University Research Computing and Classics.

docs.title: περῐ́ τῆς μηχᾰνῆς
docs.text: Hardware affects software. Logion does not require access to high-performance computing (HPC) resources. It is designed to run on local machines with divers hardware and operating systems. But although the specific hardware on which Logion runs does not affect the accuracy or quality of its predictions, hardware will affect the speed at which Logion performs its operations. Thus, for perhaps the first time in history, philologists may want to be aware of their computer's hardware. The following provides a brief overview and guide to help users navigate hardware questions specifically in relation to Logion.

docs.title: Which hardware is best?
docs.text: Hardware will not affect the accuracy or quality of predictions generated by Logion models. But hardware will affect the speed at which Logion generates predictions.

docs.title: CPU vs. GPU
docs.text: Nearly every computer nowadays has both a central processing unit (CPU) and graphics processing unit (GPU). Both are processors that use memory to store/access data, perform calculations, and execute instructions. In short, these constitute the computer's so-called brain. CPUs are responsible for general-purpose computing tasks (word processors, web browsing, etc.); GPUs are responsible for computationally expensive, high-throughput tasks (video games, training AI models, etc.).

docs.title: Does Logion use a CPU or GPU?
docs.text:
Logion is designed to run on both a local machine's CPU and GPU. When a user starts Logion, the software detects which type of GPU is available on the host machine. If the machine has a compatible GPU, it utilizes that GPU to generate any predictions. If a GPU is unavailable, Logion defaults to the machine's CPU. Our team's internal experiments suggest that, when executing Logion's error detection task on a sequence of 122 words in Byzantine Greek, a GPU speeds up performance by approximately 40% compared to the same machine's CPU. Logion is compatible with the following types of GPUs:

  • Nvidia
  • Apple Silicon (M-chip)
  • Intel Graphics

docs.title: How do I find which GPU my computer has?
On macOS
docs.text:
1. Navigate to the top-left corner of your computer's menu bar.
  2. Click the Apple Logo ().
  3. Select 'About This Mac" from the drop-down menu.
  4. Your computer's GPU should appear beside "Graphics" or "Chip".

docs.title: On Windows
docs.text:
1. Right-click on the taskbar.
  2. Select 'Task Manager'.
  3. Click the 'Performance' tab in the Task Manager window.
  4. Scroll down and select 'GPU 0'.
  5. GPU information for your computer should appear in the top-right corner.

docs.title: How Logion Works
docs.text: Logion uses language models to make suggestions for missing or alternative words in pre-modern Greek texts.

docs.title: How to Use Logion Error Detection
docs.text:
First, navigate to to the error detection window by clicking "Error Detection" on the right-hand side of the main menu.

Inside the Error Detection window, there are three main features necessary to generate possible mis-transcribed words and potential replacements for a text.

On the upper-left of the window, select which Logion model you want to use from the drop-down menu. If this is your first time, we recommend beginning with "Base BERT". This has been trained on a wide selection of pre-modern Greek and is suitable for general error-detection recommendations.

The Levenshtein distance drop-down menu is to the right of the model selection menu. Levenshtein distance is a term from natural language processing that refers to the minimum number of alterations needed to transform one word into another. As such, the selected Levenshtein distance will here limit the model's suggestions to words that have that many character-letter differences from words in the original input text. We recommend starting with a Levenshtein distance of 1.

Once you have selected your preferred model, you may type/paste your text into the text area. Unlike, Logion's word prediction feature, do NOT use "?" to represent any missing words. Error detection results are better the less missing words the input text has. Input text that contains only Greek script and punctuation.

Then, click the blue "Detect Errors" button below the text area. Note the error detection process can take several minutes depending on one's local hardware. To read more on how hardware affects error detection processing speed, click here.

Logion will display error detection results below the "Detect Errors" button. Text is color-coded to signify each given word's likelihood of it having been mis-transcribed at some point in the work's textual history. Green means the word is unlikely to be mis-transcribed, red means the word is very likely to be mis-transcribed. To see what the model suggests as the correct word, click on a given word. Alternative word suggestions will be displayed on the right-hand side of the window. You may simply click on a different word to display that word's results.

docs.title: How to Use Logion Word Prediction
docs.text:
First, navigate to to the word prediction window by clicking "Word Prediction" on the left-hand side of the main menu.

Inside the Word Prediction window, there are two main features necessary to generate single-word predictions for gaps in your text.

On the upper-left of the window, select which Logion model you want to use from the drop-down menu. If this is your first time, we recommend beginning with "Base BERT". This has been trained on a wide selection of pre-modern Greek and is suitable for general text-filling recommendations.

Once you have selected your preferred model, you may type/paste your text into the text area. Use "?" to represent any missing words for which you want to generate suggestions.

Then, click the blue "Predict" button below the text area. The model will display its word suggestions with their projected probabilities on the right-hand side of the window.
